title: Email notifications arriving hours late
body: Task assignment notifications used to arrive within a minute or two. For the past week, they've been arriving 2-4 hours late. This is causing people to miss deadlines because they don't know they've been assigned a task until hours later. It seems to be worse in the morning — notifications sent around 9-10am take the longest to arrive. Afternoon notifications are usually faster but still delayed.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report — the timing pattern you've noticed (worse in the morning, better in the afternoon) is really helpful.

You mentioned this started about a week ago. Can you think of anything that changed around that time — a new team joining TaskFlow, a change in how your team uses task assignments, or maybe a shift in when tasks are typically created? I'm trying to understand what the world looked like right before this started happening versus right after.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Hmm, I don't think our team changed anything about how we use TaskFlow. Same number of people, same workflow. But now that you mention it, I did notice we started getting these daily digest emails around the same time — like a summary of activity or something marketing-related. I think it shows up in my inbox around 9am? I'm not sure if that's related but the timing does line up with when the delays are worst. Nothing else comes to mind though.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really interesting connection — the timing of those daily digest emails lining up with the worst delays is worth pulling on.

Can you tell me more about those digest emails? For instance, does everyone on your team (or across your organization) get them, or just certain people? And do you know roughly how many users are on your TaskFlow instance? I'm wondering whether a large batch of digest emails going out at 9am might be crowding the same pipeline that sends your assignment notifications.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah, I think everyone in the organization gets them — it's not just my team. We have around 200 users on our TaskFlow instance, so that's probably a decent number of digest emails going out. I hadn't really thought about it that way but yeah, if all those digest emails are being sent at 9am, that could definitely be clogging things up. The assignment notifications used to come through almost instantly before those digests started.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Task assignment email notifications delayed 2-4 hours, likely caused by daily digest email batch saturating send pipeline","problem":"Task assignment notifications that previously arrived within 1-2 minutes are now delayed by 2-4 hours. The delays are worst in the morning (9-10am) and improve through the afternoon. This started approximately one week ago, coinciding with the introduction of daily digest/marketing summary emails sent to all users at 9am.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The daily digest email batch (sent to all ~200 users at ~9am) is being processed through the same email sending pipeline as transactional assignment notifications. The burst of ~200 digest emails saturates the queue or hits rate limits on the email provider, causing assignment notifications to back up behind them. As the digest batch drains, afternoon notifications experience shorter delays.","reproduction_steps":["Assign a task to a user at approximately 9:00-9:30am (when digest emails are being sent)","Observe that the assignment notification email arrives 2-4 hours late","Assign a task to a user in the late afternoon (after digest queue has drained)","Observe that the notification arrives faster but still with some delay"],"environment":"TaskFlow instance with approximately 200 users, daily digest emails enabled organization-wide, sending at ~9am","severity":"high","impact":"All ~200 users are affected. Delayed assignment notifications cause people to miss deadlines because they are unaware of new task assignments for hours. Morning assignments are most impacted.","recommended_fix":"Investigate the email sending architecture: (1) Determine whether digest and transactional emails share the same queue/worker pool. If so, separate them into distinct queues with transactional notifications given higher priority. (2) Check for rate limits on the email provider that the digest batch may be exhausting. (3) Consider scheduling digest emails during off-peak hours or throttling them to leave capacity for transactional sends. (4) As an immediate mitigation, consider sending digests in smaller batches spread over a longer window rather than all at once.","proposed_test_case":"Send a batch of 200 digest emails and simultaneously trigger 5 assignment notifications. Verify that assignment notifications are delivered within the expected SLA (e.g., under 2 minutes) regardless of digest batch volume. Test both with shared and separated queues to confirm the fix.","information_gaps":["Exact email infrastructure details (shared queue, provider, rate limits) — these are internal system details the reporter would not know","Whether other transactional notification types (e.g., due date reminders, comment notifications) are also delayed","Whether the digest feature was deliberately launched a week ago or appeared unexpectedly (could indicate a feature flag or deployment)"]}
